title: Misir Wot (red lentil stew)
rawBody:
- **URL:** https://www.daringgourmet.com/misir-wot-ethiopian-spiced-red-lentils/ ✓ VERIFIED
- $0.95/serving · **Menu price** $9–12 (single) / $18–22 (combo platter base) · 250 g · 340 cal · 16 g protein · 60 min · 6 servings · **Easy** · 🥘 ⚡
description: Slow-simmered red lentils in berbere-spiced caramelized-onion sauce. The Ethiopian platter's red workhorse — naturally vegan, gluten-free, tastes better day two.

title: Shiro Wot (chickpea-flour stew)
rawBody:
- **URL:** https://www.feastingathome.com/shiro-wat/ ✓ VERIFIED
- $0.85/serving · same combo placement · 240 g · 310 cal · 14 g · 35 min · 6 · **Easy** · 🥘 ⚡
description: Toasted chickpea flour whisked into berbere stew until silky. Naturally vegan and gluten-free; the creamy umami complement to spicier wots.

title: Atakilt Wat (cabbage-potato-carrot)
description: Cabbage, potato, and carrot stewed gently with turmeric and onion until everything melts together. The mild, golden anchor of any Ethiopian combo platter.

title: Kik Alicha (yellow split pea)
description: Yellow split peas simmered with turmeric, ginger, and garlic until creamy. Mellow and golden — the tame counterpart to fierier wots.

title: Gomen Wat (spicy collards)
description: Collard greens slow-cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, and a hit of jalapeño until silky. The bitter-green balance on the combo platter.

title: Authentic Vegan Injera
rawBody:
- **URL:** https://www.daringgourmet.com/injera/ ✓ VERIFIED
- $0.55/piece · **Menu price** $1.50–2.50/piece, included in combo · 110 g · 180 cal · 6 g · 3-day ferment · ~12 pieces/batch · **Medium** (ferment timing) · 🥘
description: Fermented teff sourdough flatbread — spongy, sour, naturally gluten-free. The base of every Ethiopian platter; tear, scoop, repeat.

title: Doro Wat (ያለ ጭካኔ · Yale chikane)
rawBody:
- **URL:** https://holycowvegan.net/vegan-doro-wat/ ✓ VERIFIED *(Vaishali Honawar / Holy Cow Vegan — fully vegan doro wat with tofu + mushrooms, deeply browned onions, berbere, and her vegan niter kibbeh (spiced oil substitute for the traditional ghee-based one). Updated Sep 2025; 4 servings, 250 cal, 45 min. **Recommended swap: Chunk Pulled in place of the tofu** for showstopper-tier presentation; use a whole peeled boiled potato per portion in place of the traditional hard-boiled egg. Vaishali also publishes the [berbere spice blend](https://holycowvegan.net/berbere-ethiopian-spice-blend/) and [vegan niter kibbeh](https://holycowvegan.net/vegan-niter-kibbeh-ethiopian-spiced-oil/) recipes separately — make both during prep.)*
- **Description:** **Lead protein:** Chunk Pulled in heavy berbere-spiced caramelized-onion stew, plus a whole peeled potato per portion in place of the traditional hard-boiled egg. The single most iconic dish of Ethiopian cuisine — onion is cooked low-and-slow for 90+ minutes (no oil at first, then with niter kibbeh substitute) until it nearly liquefies into the sauce. Plates as the centerpiece of an Ethiopian combination platter, or stand-alone over rice.
- **Wholesale food cost:** $4.20 / serving · **Menu price:** **$16–20** stand-alone / **$22–26** as featured center of premium combo platter
- **Weight:** 360 g · **Calories:** 480 · **Protein:** 26 g · **Time:** 2.5 hr (mostly unattended onion cook) · **Servings:** 6
- **Restaurant difficulty:** **Medium** — onion cook is long but unsupervised; everything bulk-preps and reheats with a final niter-kibbeh-substitute hit. Hold 4+ days, improves daily.
- **Tags:** 🥘 Bulk-Prep · ⚡ Fast-Service

| Sub | Effect | Cost delta |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead protein:** Chunk Pulled (default) | Closest to slow-stewed chicken texture | baseline |
| Beyond Chicken Pieces, shredded | Branded recognition | −$0.80 |
| Soy curls or seitan | Budget tier | −$2.20 → menu $13–16 |
| Whole-mushroom-and-tofu (no faux-meat) | Vegetarian-Ethiopian classic | −$2.80 → menu $12–15 |
description: **Lead protein:** Chunk Pulled in heavy berbere-spiced caramelized-onion stew, plus a whole peeled potato per portion in place of the traditional hard-boiled egg. The single most iconic dish of Ethiopian cuisine — onion is cooked low-and-slow for 90+ minutes (no oil at first, then with niter kibbeh substitute) until it nearly liquefies into the sauce. Plates as the centerpiece of an Ethiopian combination platter, or stand-alone over rice.
difficultyNote: onion cook is long but unsupervised; everything bulk-preps and reheats with a final niter-kibbeh-substitute hit. Hold 4+ days, improves daily.

title: Dabo Kolo with Cardamom-Coffee Affogato (ያለ ጭካኔ · Yale chikane)
rawTitle: ### Vegan Dabo Kolo with Cardamom-Coffee Affogato (**DESSERT**)
rawBody:
- **URL:** https://foreignfork.com/dabo-kolo/ ✓ VERIFIED *(Foreign Fork's recipe — the kolo itself is naturally vegan; the affogato pairing is our addition. Alts: https://abreadaday.com/dabo-kolo/ and https://www.fussfreecooking.com/recipe-categories/meatless-recipes/dabo-kolo-ethiopia-fried-snacks/. **Full plate-up recipe (with affogato pairing) TBD in v6.**)*
- **Description:** Crunchy hand-rolled wheat pellets sweetened with brown sugar, cardamom, and a whisper of berbere, baked until amber. Served with Ethiopian buna (coffee) and a small scoop of vegan vanilla bean ice cream — pour the espresso over for an Ethiopian-Italian affogato. Honors Ethiopia's coffee heritage; the dish feels native rather than transplanted.
- **Wholesale food cost:** $1.30 / serving (kolo + 1 scoop ice cream + buna) · **Menu price:** $7–10 (kolo solo with coffee) / $9–12 (with ice cream affogato)
- **Weight:** 140 g · **Calories:** 320 · **Protein:** 5 g · **Time:** 45 min (kolo bakes; can be made days ahead) · **Servings:** 8
- **Restaurant difficulty:** **Easy** — kolo holds 2 weeks airtight; pull ice cream, pour buna, plate.
- **Tags:** 🥘 Bulk-Prep · ⚡ Fast-Service

| Sub | Effect | Cost delta |
|---|---|---|
| Sweet kolo + buna + vegan vanilla ice cream (default) | Multi-element finish | baseline |
| Kolo only as a free post-meal nibble | Hospitality gesture, not menu | n/a |
| Add tej (Ethiopian honey wine) reduction drizzle | Premium menu listing | +$0.40 → menu $11–14 |
description: Crunchy hand-rolled wheat pellets sweetened with brown sugar, cardamom, and a whisper of berbere, baked until amber. Served with Ethiopian buna (coffee) and a small scoop of vegan vanilla bean ice cream — pour the espresso over for an Ethiopian-Italian affogato. Honors Ethiopia's coffee heritage; the dish feels native rather than transplanted.
menuPrice: $7–10 (kolo solo with coffee) / $9–12 (with ice cream affogato)

Kolo only as a free post-meal nibble
Add tej (Ethiopian honey wine) reduction drizzle
